author: @musikSkool
text: The best we can hope for is predictable results.  However, quite a lot of science right now, especially in medicine, refuses huge amounts of data on the basis that you just have to trust them, regardless what the results actually are.

author: @Pullen-Paradox
text: I think that the average person is essentially good and essentially evil. How in the world can I think this? I'm not sure exactly, but I believe that people are generally good when they are in interaction with other humans, and often the opposite when they are thinking about other people but are not confronted with them. there is love, kindness, charity when a person is confronted with the fact of another person; there is suspicion, envy, and feelings of inferiority when not thinking about others but not confronted with them.

author: @frankbenevolence
text: I think a love of truth (Philo Sophie) and to help others escape misguided beliefs are good enough reason to challenge unexamined beliefs.  The challenging of moral principles is how humanity has progressed from cannibalistic barbarians to the space faring cosmopolitan world of today.  We are the same animal, the training has gotten better.

author: @orpheuscreativeco9236
text:
[I] have been wrestling with the idea that everything is of a singular consciousness in this universe and that everything (perceived as individual) is merely a permutation of exploration, an experiment of that singular consciousness. Even though we are physically individual we are just unconscious of our connectivity, our oneness with all. When we engage with anyone or anything outside of ourselves we only come to understand more about the way its all tied together.   Just thoughts though, I haven't read on subject. Maybe its time to start 😁👍

Thanks for sharing Rob! ✌️

author: @ourkilkennyhomestead2006
text: Howdy rob, great video. Fully sums up the workings of the analytic mind in a well worded piece. I must say I do envy your ability to adequately verbalise the mental workings of individuals such as ourselves so well. Not even going to attempt to match that in commentary form. All I can say is "spot on sir", absolutely spot on!

author: @siowat7911
text: Tom Holland, the historian not the actor, has written a book called 'Dominion' in which he traces the origins of his beliefs and finds that most of them go back to Jesus Christ. I was an atheist up until the age of 47, but I am now a Christian due largely to experiences that psychologists would refer to as synchronicity. There were just too many of these "synchronizations" for me to believe they were chance. Also I had what could be described as supernatural experiences. As a Christian I believe in shining the light of Jesus into the world in the hope that others will come to the light and salvation, but I also believe that God gave us all freewill and we get to choose who we follow (very different to Islam).

author: @supergobblin
text:
As you know I love your channel and you (I'm wearing my "be a maker not a taker" mask!), and am not a know-it-all (I've learned so much from you!).   That said, without trying to convince you of anything really, I can suggest this simple truth the denial of which I think you'll agree would be nonsense:

 that you think you know what you're talking about.

Otherwise I suppose you wouldn't say anything.   I hope this is the case because, suppose you're wrong and you've led people down the path to hell?  (I don't believe this, we are all responsible for our own decisions, but certainly I could be wrong if you could be.)


the consequences of this little fact (that there are truths) are played out in some detail in a book I wrote in an attempt to have the world's longest title, it's on amazon  but I'm not selling books and am happy to send you (or anyone) a PDF if you like:

https://www.amazon.com/Words-Foundationalism-Materialism-Communication-Post-modernism-ebook/dp/B0B1LYDC33/

In short, though, philosophy -is- a science but just as in all sciences, solutions long lost are sometimes the best.

Best Regards,

RL
